Montréal, Québec

Date of decision
17 octobre 2012
Tribunal
Panel
Sylvie Roy
Conseil du demandeur d'asile
Claimant's Counsel
Warren L Creates
Agent du tribunal
Tribunal Officer
Myriam De Bailleul
[Dépôt de documents]
Représentant désigné
Designated representative
XXXX XXXX XXXX
Conseil du ministre
Minister's Counsel
S/O
INTRODUCTION
[1] XXXX XXXX XXXX (hereafter called principal claimant), her spouse, XXXX XXXX XXXX (hereafter called spouse), and their minor children, XXXX XXXX XXXX and XXXX XXXX XXXX claim refugee protection pursuant to sections 96, membership in a particular social group, Yibir clan, and 97(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act1 (IRPA).
[2] The principle claimant was appointed as the designated representative of her children.
SUMMARY OF ALLEGATIONS
Principal claimant
[3] According to the claimants' written statements found within their Personal Information Forms (PIFS), the principal claimant is a member of a minority tribe in Djibouti (Yibir tribe) while her spouse belongs to the Issa clan, a majority clan. They claim that they suffered persecution at the hands of all Djibouti nationals as a result of their mixed marriage.
[4] The principal claimant outlines in detail in her PIF that her extended family (she has 10 siblings) have suffered persecution in the form of insults and humiliation and physical attacks for as long as she can remember. She relates that a first marriage in 1999 to a man from the Issaack tribe resulted in divorce two years later because his family never accepted the marriage.
[5] She nevertheless married outside of her clan again in XXXX 2007; her spouse was represented at the marriage ceremony by two paternal uncles while her brothers represented her. She and her husband settled in Quartier XXXX of Djibouti, the capital city. From the beginning of the marriage, neighbors began insulting the couple and treating her badly.
[6] When she completed her XXXX XXXX in 2003, she immediately began XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. She claims that she wasn't properly paid and as a result her husband needed to work in the evenings to supplement their income. She also suffered abuse from the XXXX XXXX, particularly when some of XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. She remembered one parent slapping her cheek while another spat on her.
[7] The final incidents that drove them out of Djibouti occurred on XXXX XXXX, 2008 and XXXX XXXX, 2010. In 2008, the spouse claims that armed men, two of which she recognized as leaders in a gang in her neighborhood attacked her and her infant daughter. They destroyed the baby's room and threw a rock on her head and kicked her stomach. XXXX, another neighbor brought the spouse and her daughter to the hospital, which resulted in getting two sutures on her forehead.
[8] On XXXX XXXX, 2009, while pregnant again, the family decided to move to another municipality within the capital, called XXXX, in a house that her husband had inherited from his father. The claimants allege that the violence toward the principal claimant intensified in that neighborhood. After their son's birth, on XXXX XXXX, 2010, six men, including an Issa "chef", by the name of XXXX, dragged her outside of her home and tied her to a post naked and kicked her in the stomach. The back of the house was destroyed and their belongings burned. Her daughter was hit by a flying rock. XXXX warned that the next time, the family would be killed. Both were hospitalized in XXXX and the daughter had two sutures in her face.
Spouse
[9] The spouse relates that he led a quiet life with his family before he married. He dedicated his life to his XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX and became a XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXXin Djibouti.
[10] After he married, often the authorities of Djibouti suggested that he end his marriage because his marriage to a Yibir tarnished his public reputation as a XXXX XXXX and his image as a XXXX XXXX of Djibouti. He alleges that his salary was often frozen as a result of his marriage.
[11] The claimants left Djibouti, with their genuine passports issued in XXXX and XXXX 2010, and American visas issued in XXXX 2010. A friend of the family who lived in XXXX in Pennsylvania had invited the family and they remained at her home while in the United States for 19 days.
[12] They claimed refugee protection at Lacolle on XXXX XXXX XXXX 2010.
ANALYSE
Identity
[13] The claimants' identity as nationals of Djibouti is established by copies of their passports filed under Exhibit A-2, Immigration documents.
[14] The panel gives the benefit of the doubt to the claimants and accepts that the principal claimant is from the Yibir clan and that her spouse is of the Issa clan.
Credibility
[15] However, the panel did not find the claimants credible. The following are examples that led the panel to question the veracity of their story.
[16] The panel finds it helpful to briefly outline what is revealed about the Yibir clan in the documentary evidence.
[17] The document evidence 2states:
"4. Minorities
Minorities are not clans, although this is what the Somali Nomadic clans call them because they want to assimilate them into their structure. Among the minorities, one can find the 'outcaste' groups, or bondsmen known collectively as sab, as well as groups of ethnic Bantu descent and the coastal groups, including those of Arabic descent such as the Bajunis and Barawanis. Minorities are not counted and their languages and cultures are neither accepted nor respected.
It should be first noted that being a minority does not reveal whether or not one is at risk of becoming targeted. Secondly, in terms of their size, the notion of "minorities" is sometimes misleading. Many minorities, such as Bantus, are in many places in South Central Somalia in fact local majorities. However, they are being oppressed by the militarily stronger nomadic clans. Globally, in the Somali context, they are a minority, because they are not overall dominant. The sab are an exception to this, as they are in numbers a clear-cut minority due to the fact that, unlike the Bantus, who live in certain locations, they are scattered over many places.
Thirdly, one can observe the reverse situation in the case of clan groups (such as the Biymaal) who in some areas live in pockets of groups and thus can be referred to as "minorities" on the local level with some justification, but not on the global Somali level due to the fact that they belong to a strong clan-family. Hence generally they can leave the area where they constitute a "minority" and receive protection where their clan is a majority (even though the notion of being "dominant" nowhere means full control, as there are always several clans, and "minorities" present in South Central Somalia). However, this often means that these groups are obliged to leave their local areas where they probably have been living for generations.
This being said, it should be noted that such generalised knowledge and information on clans and minorities is of very limited use when it comes to assessing an individual's risk. This is particularly true in asylum procedures, where decisions made on basis of generalizations of the conditions of certain groups can lead to asylum decisions made on false premises. It must therefore be emphasized that all asylum cases should be treated as individual cases....
Yibr
The Yibr or Yibro (some find Yibro is a mispronunciation) live along the coast in Mogadishu and in Bosasso, Borama, and Burco. In the South, they are described as being distinct from Gabooye. The Yibr are often claimed to be descendants of early Hebrews who settled in the Horn of Africa. According to Virginia Luling, the 'Hebrew' idea is not an anti-Semitic invention by others, but was and is maintained by the Yibr themselves, who have found in this a way of dignifying their outcast status. However, while this Hebrew descendancy is disputed, it is not entirely baseless. While it is true that the 1970's produced a myth about their descent, which was related to a political effort at discrediting them by playing on anti-Semitic sentiments, there is some historical merit to the notion that ancient Somali people prior to Islam and Christianity did have a connection to Jewish or similar religions, and the present group may derive from the 'losing' part in an ancient religious dispute at Aw-Barkhadle (a religious centre commemorating Sheikh Yusuf Al-Kownin) in present day Somaliland, which still is a location of pilgrimage located between Hargeysa and Berbera. This, however, needs more research outside our present scope.
Members of Yibr used to have mythological functions in society (and do not involve themselves in other tasks in traditional Somali society): They collected the Samanyo (a birth gift) from new-born babies and newly-married girls in exchange for giving them a good fortune. Historically, the Yibr enjoyed some protection before independence through this superstitious practice that prevailed about them, and which is now no longer widely practiced. After independence they suffered from the banning of the Samanyo custom and other related traditions by the government.
With the presence of radical Islamic groups like Al-Shabaab with strong anti-Jewish attitudes, the Yibr who claim historical descent from the Hebrews have been increasingly suspected by Somalis with a radical Islamic orientation. Therefore members of the Yibr may be targeted in South Central Somalia, despite the fact that they are Muslims today."
[18] Other documentary evidence3 states:
" Sources have identified the boon caste not only as a separate sab group, or occupational grouping, of Somalia but also report that they are composed of a variety of different groups, such as the Yibir, Midgaan, Madiban, Tumal, Yahar and Yihir clans, as well as being another name for the Midgan.
Traditionally, the occupational groups could not own land or livestock and work opportunities were mainly limited to menial jobs or specific trades. Intermarriage is not permitted or accepted between sab minorities and the "noble" nomadic clans. In respect of language, the Ethnologue: Languages of the World notes that the Boon language is nearly extinct.
According to 1997 Canadian Immigration and Refugee Board information, people who are generally referred to as boon are those members of clans who, over a period of time, were adopted into another clan and are seen to be members proper of that clan but remain despised because of their lowly or unknown origins. Although in much of the inter-riverine area of Somalia the boon is treated as a uniform category of people, they are in fact composed of a broad range of different groups. Ethnologue: Languages of the World notes that the boon included the Yibir, Midgaan, Madiban, Tumal, Yahar and Yihir clans. The UNHCR notes that the Midgan are also collectively referred to as Mahdiban, Boon or Gaboye, depending on the location. In an Austrian Centre for Country of Origin & Asylum Research and Documentation (ACCORD) report the Boon were identified as a separate sab minority present in the south of Somalia. In addition, a minority which may have an ethnic origin distinct from samaal, such as a slavery heritage, are called various derogatory names, such as boon (person of low status) or addoon (slave), by other Somali clans.
The term sab refers collectively to "outcaste" groups (bondsmen) and is often used to refer to minority groups identified in occupational terms. According to the Minority Rights Group (MRG) "outcaste" groups are now known as "occupational groups". The MRG states that occupational groups historically comprise three main minorities: the Midgan (now commonly known as Gaboye, Madhiban and Musse Deriyo), Tumal and Yibro. These occupational groups are scattered throughout Somalia (Somaliland and Puntland), Ethiopia and Djibouti."
[19] An article published by the British Broadcasting Corporation (12 December 2006) in Exhibit C-18, states that "Marriage is the area where traditional prejudices remain strongest, with men who marry lower caste women often ostracized by their families."
[20] A Response to Information Request, Number Dji31462.E found in Exhibit C-19, states in part:
"...
The Djibouti legal system is based upon French law and Islamic law. However for the family matters such as marriages, divorces, inheritance, formal custody of the children, etc., only Islamic law [Sharia] is competent [has precedence] (both sources). While mixed marriages are frequent in Djibouti society, those between members of the Midgan and members of other clans, including Issa, are rare among Djibouti people (Djibouti lawyer). However marriages between members of the Midgan clan and members of others clans is not prohibited by the law (ibid.). The few cases of those marriages are registered in Djibouti town, where traditions are less strong than in the countryside (Djibouti lawyer). As Djibouti society is patrilineal, children from all mixed marriages always belong to their father's clan (both sources). The two sources indicated that, in case of the death of the father, the formal authority over children including those from mixed marriage is generally assumed by the mother. However custody can be allowed to one of the close members of the father's family (generally to one of the father' brothers) if the children's mother is incapable (ibid.). The sources added that the incapacity of the mother is established by the Cadi [Judge]."
[21] Quite clearly, the documentary evidence cited above establishes that the Yibir is an "outcaste" groups (bondsmen) and is often used to refer to minority groups identified in occupational terms" and suffer discrimination and in some cases, persecution.
[22] In Rajudeen, the Federal Court adopted the following definitions of persecution:
"To harass or afflict with repeated acts of cruelty or annoyance; to afflict or punish because of particular opinions or adherence to a particular creed or mode of worship...
A particular course or period of systematic infliction of punishment directed against those holding a particular (religious belief); persistent injury or annoyance from any source.4 "
[23] Professor Hathaway describes persecution in the following terms:
"... persecution may be defined as the sustained or systemic violation of basic human rights demonstrative of a failure of state protection. A well-founded fear of persecution exists when one reasonably anticipates that remaining in the country may result in a form of serious harm which government cannot or will not prevent, including either "specific hostile acts or ... an accumulation of adverse circumstances such as discrimination existing in an atmosphere of insecurity and fear.5 "
[24] The alleged experiences described by the principal claimant in paragraphs 1 thru 8 do not constitute persecution. Although the claimant may have been subjected to discriminatory acts, for example, the altercation that occurred at XXXX in 1998 and the resulting punishment of being banned from XXXXfor 10 days, the refusal of her ex-laws to welcome her in their family and the ensuing insults, the panel finds that they are not serious enough to constitute persecution. In order to be considered persecution, the mistreatment suffered or anticipated must be serious, i.e., it must constitute a key denial of a core human right6.
[25] Furthermore, the panel does not believe the allegations of the more serious incidents as related in paragraphs 9 thru 14 for the following reasons.
[26] The principal claimant submitted often that she was hated and persecuted because she was a Yibir. The panel questioned the principal claimant as to how the community became aware that she was in fact a Yibir. She responded in general terms that Djibouti is a small country and that the community simply became aware. This explanation did not satisfy the panel for the following reasons.
[27] Her father was a XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX while her mother was a XXXX. Her nine siblings attended the Lycée level in school; one brother XXXX XXXX XXXX in Djibouti, one is a XXXX XXXX, while another is a XXXX. She could not remember what the occupations of her other siblings.
[28] First, the principal claimant presented herself as a sophisticated and educated woman, who has XXXX years of schooling in Djibouti. She attended the "XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX" and obtained the necessary qualifications to XXXX XXXX XXXX in her country in 2003. In fact, she was hired by XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, a position she retained until she left Djibouti in XXXX 2010.
[29] The documentary evidence7 states:
"In 2004/05 primary enrolment included 33% of pupils in the relevant age-group (36% of boys; 29% of girls), 82 schools with 46,523 pupils; and secondary enrolment was equivalent to 19% of pupils in the relevant age-group (22% of boys; 15% of girls); First cycle: 17,180 pupils, second cycle, 6,767 pupils. Higher education (2004-2005): 1,746 students."
[30] Plainly, the principal claimant was part of a tiny group of Djiboutiennes who possessed the privilege of attending post-secondary education in her country. The XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX produces only XXXX XXXX XXXX. According to the principal claimant, she was only one of two students from a minority clan admitted to the school. According to her counsel, she evidently worked hard and obtained the marks required to earn admittance to the school. The significant factor here is that she was indeed admitted to the XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX in spite of being a Yibir.
[31] As well in 2003, the principal claimant attended a private school, the Lycée de XXXX XXXXDjibouti, where she met her current spouse. Her spouse testified that the monthly cost to attend that particular private school at the time was XXXX XXXX XXXX Djibouti francs monthly, which is equivalent to about XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXXUS.
[32] The average salary in Djibouti is 331,000 Djibouti francs, which is equivalent to about $1,879.00US per year. Clearly, if this couple had the means to attend a private school in 2003-2005 and 2000-2003 respectively, their income was, on a balance of probabilities, higher than the average salary paid in the country.
[33] The principal claimant's pay stub for the month of XXXX 2010 is included in the Immigration documents, Exhibit A-2. Her gross monthly salary, which included a "Presta Familial of 1,400 Djibouti francs and an "Ind logement" of 50,000 Djibouti francs was 156,150 Djibouti francs which is equivalent to 863US monthly income or 10,356US yearly and a net income of 140,654 Djibouti francs, which is equivalent to 777US monthly or 9,324US yearly income. The "Certificate de Travail" establishes that she was an "XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. Clearly, her salary reflected that it climbed up the grid.
[34] The spouse neglected to include his employment as a XXXXemployee, "XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXXen qualité de XXXX XXXX XXXX" at question 7 of his PIF. Questioned about his employment, he testified that his sole employment was as an XXXX XXXX XXXX. Confronted with a copy of a pay stub8 and a copy of a "XXXX XXXX9, the spouse bellowed that he had forgotten about that particular employment. Given that the documents were dated XXXX and XXXX 2010, the month prior to the claimants' departure from Djibouti; the panel finds the spouse's explanation about the oversight not credible. Although neither document mentions the length of service as a XXXXemployee, the pay stub reveals that the spouse was receiving a tenure (seniority) monthly bonus of 4,204 Djibouti francs, which leads the panel to conclude, on a balance of probabilities that he had been working at that XXXX for a lengthy period. As a "XXXX", he received an annual gross income of 1,311,648 (109,304 x 12) Djibouti francs which is equivalent to or 897,624 (74,802 x 12) net annual income.
[35] Both claimants testified that the spouse also worked as a XXXX in the evenings to supplement their income. He would XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. He was known as a XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX" in his country. Although information about his income from this work was not canvassed, clearly it was relevant since he testified that given his XXXX, XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX.
[36] The documentary evidence mentioned above states that the unemployment rate in 2004/2005 in Djibouti was over 40%; the principal claimant herself rated it higher in testimony. 12.5% of the population experience severe poverty while 18.8% live below the poverty line.
[37] There is clearly no reliable evidence in these claims that the claimants were discriminated on the basis of ethnicity in employment and job advancement.
[38] Again, it is abundantly clear that this family was amongst the privileged citizens of Djibouti with respect to their standards of living and it did not reflect the profile of a Yibir and/or a mixed marriage where traditional prejudices and ostracism exist as described in the documentary evidence.
[39] The claimants also provided an "Acte Charien de Mariage" in Exhibit A-2, Immigration Documents. Nowhere is it stated on the document that this marriage is a mixed marriage. As well, the ethnicity of each spouse is not indicated on the document. Oddly, the document mentions that the principal claimant was a virgin at the time of this marriage. This led the panel to question whether she had indeed been married once before.
[40] The documentary evidence mentioned above, state that :
"...marriages between members of the Midgan clan and members of others clans is not prohibited by the law (ibid.). The few cases of those marriages are registered in Djibouti town, where traditions are less strong than in the countryside (Djibouti lawyer)." There was no evidence to demonstrate that this particular mixed marriage was registered as such in Djibouti town, which begs the question again, how did the general population recognize the principal claimant as a Yibir married to an Issa?
[41] The panel found these facts disturbing and led to its disbelief of the incidents that allegedly led the claimants to leave Djibouti, that is, the allegations of XXXX XXXX, 2008 XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX 2010 for the following reasons.
[42] Given the allegations, the principal claimant was asked whether the police were called on those particular evenings. After proclaiming that the police were never helpful in the past, she acknowledged that she did not report the incidents to the authorities. Given her testimony that a neighbor brought them to the hospital and that her daughter received medical care in XXXX 2008, she was asked whether she attempted to obtain a medical report from the hospital in question before leaving Djibouti or since her arrival in Canada. She responded that she did not think about asking for such documents.
[43] These claimants entered Canada with numerous documents. They travelled with birth certificates, two marriage certificates, national identity cards, a voting card, and statutory declaration of the principal claimant's brother who had been accepted as a refugee in Canada, his PIF and Permanent Residency Card, numerous copies of school records and employment documents and pay stubs. Identity documents are significant in refugee claims. However, these claimants also possessed genuine passports from their country of nationality.
[44] Given her level of education, her demonstrated resourcefulness and finally given that she and family were represented by an experience counsel before the Board, the claimants' lack of effort to obtain documents from the hospital and/or doctor who allegedly cared for the daughter to corroborate her story impacts negatively on their credibility.
[45] With respect to the XXXX XXXX, 2010, incident, the principal claimant testified that once again, her spouse was absent from the home; while living in Djibouti, her husband often XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. She testified that when she discovered her daughter covered in blood, she fainted. When she opened her eyes, her daughter had been brought back from the hospital by her husband (he had been called to come home from his engagement) and her mother was also at the home. Questioned about her injuries, she testified that she had a few bruises, no serious wounds.
[46] Given her testimony, the principal claimant was asked to explain the inconsistency between her written narrative and her testimony. In her written narrative, she states "Nous fumes ramener à l'hôpital de XXXX". In testimony, she stated that her spouse brought her daughter to the hospital. She explained that she was referring to her spouse and her daughter when she said "Nous". The explanation was unsatisfactory to the panel for the following reason. The word "Nous" in French translates to the word "We" in English. The Collins Dictionary defines the word "We" as "A pronoun refers to the speaker or writer and another person". The principal claimant is a XXXX, with at least 7 years' experience. It is not credible that she was referring to her spouse and daughter in the context of her story. The inconsistency between her narrative and her testimony about who attended the hospital further impacts negatively on credibility.
[47] Yet again, there was no medical certificate to establish that she and her daughter suffered physical attacks on XXXX XXXX XXXX 2010, or any other documents to establish that their home was attacked and partly burned or that their belongings were destroyed.
[48] Given that she recognized XXXX as one of her aggressors and that the home was allegedly ransacked, she was asked if she reported the attack to the authorities. Again, she testified that she had not.
[49] Of course, the panel acknowledges that the absence of documents to corroborate the claimants' story, in of itself, is not determinative. But in the context of the stories in their entirety, it is not credible that this family was discriminated against or persecuted and the absence of documents becomes significant. The following analysis supports further this finding.
[50] In the written narratives, it was alleged that the family moved to XXXX to flee discrimination and persecution because of the principal claimant's clan. The spouse testified that the authorities harassed him to end his relationship with his wife and that his salary was often frozen. In contrast, he labeled himself as a star performer in his country, often performing for the authorities and this until the period he left Djibouti. With respect to his salary having been frozen, there was no documentary evidence to establish that aspect of his claim. Contrary to his testimony, the one pay stub he brought with him to Canada establishes clearly that he was even paid a monthly bonus for tenure at his government job. The principal claimant was also paid a benefit for lodging. Do these facts demonstate persecution? The panel finds that they prove the contrary. They were well treated by the government, at least, at their employment.
[51] The spouse testified that he is certain that he and his family will be persecuted as a result of leaving their country, should they return to Djibouti. There is no independent and reliable documentary evidence to support this allegation.
[52] In assessing the claimant's credibility, the panel also considered the consistency of his allegations to the documentary evidence. Claimants may have a subjective fear that they will be persecuted if returned to their country, but the fear must be assessed objectively in light of the situation in the country of which claimants are nationals to determine whether it is well-founded. Both the subjective fear and the objective basis are crucial elements.
[53] In this regard, the Board's country documents and the documents provided by counsel fail to provide any corroborative evidence that similarly situated persons to the claimant have faced persecution, as alleged by the spouse.
[54] The documentary evidence10 states that "The law generally provides for freedom of movement within the country, foreign travel, emigration, and repatriation, and the government generally respected these rights in practice."
[55] The claimant, while in Canada, XXXX XXXX XXXX11 criticizing the ruling government. Although XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. The spouse is afraid that the Djibouti government will discover XXXX XXXX and persecute him as a result should he and his family return to their country.
[56] Asked whether he XXXX XXXX XXXX while in Djibouti, the spouse replied that he would not have even thought about doing that in his country for fear of being persecuted by the authorities.
[57] Hence, the fact that the claimants XXXX XXXX XXXX before their refugee claims being finalized, in the panel's opinion, further demonstrate the lack of subjective fear in returning to his country.
[58] Item 80 of the Handbook on Procedures and Criteria for Determining Refugee Status under the 1951 Convention and the 1967 Protocol Relating to the Status of Refugees states the following:
"Holding political opinions different from those of the Government is not in itself a ground for claiming refugee status and an applicant must show that he has a fear of persecution for holding such opinions. This presupposes that the applicant holds opinions not tolerated by the authorities, which are critical of their policies or methods. It also presupposes that such opinions have come to the notice of the authorities or are attributed by them to the applicant."
[59] The panel was unable to find evidence to establish that returnees who have expressed a political opinion outside of their country are persecuted upon their return to Djibouti. Consequently, the panel finds, on a balance of probabilities, that the claimants will not be persecuted as a result of XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX should they return to their country.
[60] The panel concludes on a balance of probabilities that the claimants fabricated incidents of persecution to bolster refugee claims. They are not credible witnesses.
Witness
[61] The witness, who is an older brother, of the principal claimant, was found to be a Convention refugee in XXXX 2008, after having fled Djibouti in XXXX 2007. The panel is not bound by the decision of another Board member.
[62] However, his PIF was entered as Exhibit C-2. He testified that the principal claimant's story was identical to his story of persecution as a Yibir in Djibouti. The panel affords little weight to the witness's testimony for the following reasons.
[63] First, the claims are distinguishable in that the witness was never hired by a XXXX XXXX. Secondly, in his PIF, the witness indicated that he left the family home in XXXX 1999, at which time the principal claimant was a fourteen year-old child. Based on his evidence that he was out of the family home in 1999 and out of the country in 2007, he had no first-hand knowledge of the events alleged in this claim. He conceded that at the outset of his testimony. He presented no impartial evidence to establish independently the alleged discrimination and persecution. His understanding of the events was conveyed to him by the claimants.
Subjective fear
[64] The Board also concludes that the claimants did not demonstrate a subjective fear of persecution. The claimant travelled to the United States of America, with American visas, without making refugee claims in that country. Given that they had a family friend who had been long-time resident of the United States, the principal claimant was asked why they did not claim refugee status in the United States during their 19-day stay. The principal claimant testified that it was always their intention to join her brother in Canada. She added that she and her family could not speak English and reiterated that she wanted to be with her brother in Canada, who would be willing to assist them.
[65] In Hibo Farah Mohamed et Ardo Abdi Omer c. M.C.I., the Court concluded :
" The Geneva Convention exists for persons who require protection and not to assist persons who simply prefer asylum in one country over another. The Convention and the Immigration Act should be interpreted with the correct purpose in mind12. "
[66] In Assadi, Nasser-Eddin, the Court found :
" Failure to immediately seek protection can impugn the claimant's credibility, including his or her testimony about events in his country of origin13. "
[67] Their failure to make a refugee claim in the in the Unites States, a country that is signatory to the Refugee Convention, through which they visited relatives on their way to Canada, and their substantive delay in leaving that country clearly demonstrates a clear lack of subjective fear.
[68] As a result of the above analysis of credibility concerns, the panel finds that central portions of the claimants' allegations are not believable.
[69] Based on the inconsistencies, the panel finds the claimants not to be credible and trustworthy witnesses with respect to central elements of their refugee claims, particularly the principal claimant's alleged problems of persecution at the hands of physical aggressors, due to their membership to the Yibir tribe. The Court of Appeal stated in Sheikh:14 "even without disbelieving every word ... ?a claimant? has uttered, a ... panel may reasonably find him so lacking in credibility that it concludes there is no credible evidence relevant to his claim."
[70] Furthermore, case law has established that a claimant's contradictory testimony can cast doubt upon the totality of his or her viva voce evidence. In Dan-Ash,15 Mr. Justice Hugessen stated:
". . . . unless one is prepared to postulate (and accept) unlimited credulity on the part of the Board, there must come a point at which a witness's contradictions will move even the most generous trier of fact to reject his evidence."
[71] In light of the panel's finding on the credibility of the claimants, the panel determines that there is not a serious possibility of persecution should they return to Djibouti nor on the balance of probabilities that they would face a risk to life or a risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment or would face a danger of torture based on the principal claimant's membership of the Yibir tribe.
CONCLUSION
For the foregoing reasons, the panel concludes that the claimants are not "Convention refugees" and not "persons in need of protection". The panel therefore rejects all four claims.

1 " 97. (1) A person in need of protection is a person in Canada whose removal to their country or countries of nationality or, if they do not have a country of nationality, their country of former habitual residence, would subject them personally
(a) to a danger, believed on substantial grounds to exist, of torture within the meaning of Article 1 of the Convention Against Torture; or
(b) to a risk to their life or to a risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment if
(i) the person is unable or, because of that risk, unwilling to avail themself of the protection of that country,
(ii) the risk would be faced by the person in every part of that country and is not faced generally by other individuals in or from that country,
(iii) the risk is not inherent or incidental to lawful sanctions, unless imposed in disregard of accepted international standards, and
(iv) the risk is not caused by the inability of that country to provide adequate health or medical care."
